This is tricky, because Adobe's use of the BOM may be subtly different than the canonical use.

Let's start with the decoder.  The decoder looks at the first two bytes to make a decision.  As Adobe says, "This mechanism precludes beginning a string using PDFDocEncoding with the two characters `thorn` `ydieresis`, which is unlikely to be a meaningful beginning of a word or phrase."

When the decoder sees those two bytes, it decodes using UTF-16, but, most importantly, it _discards the two bytes before doing the decode_, which means that for full transparency and
ability to round-trip properly, the encoder has to add those two bytes when encoding a UTF-16 string.

But, just as importantly, the encoder needs to insure that those two bytes are not present at the start of a string that is _not_ encoded with UTF-16.

So, if the source Unicode starts with `U+FEFF`, you are absolutely right that the encoder must use UTF-16.  OTOH, the encoder must use UTF-16 if that character code (or any other character code not supported by pdfdocencoding) appears _anywhere_ in the string.  This is enforced by the exception raised by `encode('pdfdocencoding')`. 

But `thorn` and `ydieresis` are legal characters in a PDFDocEncoding string, except together right at the start.  So if a source string starts with these two characters, then for the string to be properly encoded, an additional FEFF must be prepended, and it must be encoded in UTF-16 mode.

That check is what this constant is used for.

Having said all that, since it _is_ very confusing, maybe having BOM in the name is incorrect and part of the problem.  We could rename the constant to something like `escape_check` or anything else that might make sense to you...

FWIW, I have added tests to insure that the round-trip is happening properly.  If you change this constant (and fwiw, Python won't let you change it to the real Unicode BOM, but to check that the test does something useful, you could change it to something like u'your bom here'), you will see that it creates test failures in test_pdfstring.py.

I think there are a few possible approaches (assuming embedding `jwks.json` in the codebase, which I think is a sensible tradeoff):

1. (the approach used here previously) for each incoming auth request, check if the jwks is cached, if so use that, otherwise load from disk, and then store to cache.
1. (the approach used after the PR update) for each incoming auth request, load jwks from disk
1. at process start, load the jwks into a constant at the top of this file, which is referenced by each request

These all have different trade-offs:
* Approach 1 makes a roundtrip to the remote Redis instance (it's not a localhost server or Django's locmem cache) for every request, to fetch a constant. Every N minutes (where N defaults to 5) it would have a cache miss and have to hit the disk again. 
* Approach 2 hits the disk for each request (the Heroku dynos most likely aren't great for disk I/O)
* Approach 3 means keeping the key permanently in memory (but given it's tiny I don't think it's even worth worrying about)

Approach 3 was what I was meaning above (I probably could have been clearer - sorry!) - but open to ideas? :-)

> Is calling the var \_\_version\_\_ conventional?

Yes, see https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0396/#specification

> 3. When a module (or package) includes a version number, the version SHOULD be available in the \_\_version\_\_ attribute.

---

> This would seem to mean that setup.py needs to be installed along with, e.g., cs50.py itself. Is that indeed the norm with PyPi packages, does setup.py end up getting installed somewhere?

The `setup.py` script end up being part of the source distribution (sdist) that we build and deploy to PyPI, since `pip` uses it to build this package for and install it on the target system. I don't think it typically gets installed (it's not in our case either), but what does get installed is some metadata about the package, based on what we provided in `setup.py`, and that's what's used to extract the version number at runtime in this case.

---

> Separately, what's to be the relationship between the version number in setup.py and our GitHub repo's branches? Should we automatically (forcibly) create a GitHub tag and release called v#.#.# anytime there's a push, extracting the release's version number from setup.py too?

If you're referring to the problem per https://github.com/cs50/submit50/pull/44, recall PyPI doesn't allow the same filename (including version) to be uploaded more than once. So we can't re-upload the same version of a package, to overwrite it, for example. (And frankly, this doesn't seem ideal, since two users might have the same package version installed, but effectively, they're different packages.)

But it might be the case that we, for example, have multiple PRs pending, and we want some or all of them to be part of the next release. If we just go ahead and merge these PRs into master, one after the other, only the first one will be deployed to PyPI, and all the others will fail, because they use the same version number. So we can't do that, using the current setup, without merging these PRs into one, and then merging that big PR into master, which seems unnecessary.

For that reason, we should automatically deploy, but manually trigger deployment somehow. A couple of solutions to this might be:

 1. Deploy on tags instead of commits to master. This should allow us to merge as many PRs as we want before the next release is published. And once we're comfortable releasing, we just tag and push that tag, which should trigger a build and deploy to PyPI.

 2. Deploy on commits to a production branch (or the equivalent). This also should allow us to merge as many PRs as we want into master, without any problems. And once a release is ready, we merge master into production, which should trigger a build and deploy to PyPI.

If we're going to store the `repo` and `gitref` in the url, we should remove it from the `ui` state. I.e., we want a single source of truth.

To handle this, I think we have access to a `url` prop for our `Page` component as documented in https://github.com/zeit/next.js/#shallow-routing.

I think the appropriate flow would be the following (not thinking about server failures yet...):

1. User submits binder form
2. We push a change to the url (shallow route)
3. `componentWillReceiveProps` notices a change to the `repo` and/or `gitref` (might even want to store these together as a single query param for clarity) and dispatches an action to create a new server connection.

```
    An infinite sequence will prevent the rest of the arguments from
    being included.

    We use chain.from_iterable rather than ``chain(*seqs)`` so that seqs
    can be a generator.

    >>> list(concat([[], [1], [2, 3]]))
    [1, 2, 3]

    See also:
        itertools.chain.from_iterable  equivalent
    """
    return itertools.chain.from_iterable(seqs)



def concatv(*seqs):
    """ Variadic version of concat

    >>> list(concatv([], ["a"], ["b", "c"]))
    ['a', 'b', 'c']

    See also:
        itertools.chain
    """
    return concat(seqs)

```

I know, this is just a conftest function, however:

I took a different approach regarding passing the amount. In Javascript, every amount now being passed to some function needs to be of type string, to avoid integer overflows on the user-side:


```python
# python max integer (within the range of asset.amount's max value)
>>> sys.maxint
9223372036854775807
```

```javascript
// js max integer (not within the range of asset.amount's max value)
Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER // 9007199254740991
```

Since Python's max integer is well within asset.amount's max value, we can still pass integers to user-facing functions, where as in Javascript we should always fall back to strings or even an extra library (e.g. big.js).


Just wanted to share that information.

I started with it that way earlier, but it was erroneous in that it will invoke *explicit* conversions as well, and that could give a different conversion path than you would get in regular C++ code.

For example, in the example classes in example18.cpp, Ex18_B is implicitly convertible to Ex18_E uniquely, via its `operator Ex18_E()`.  Without the construct-and-move approach, you (can) also get the temporary Ex18_E via either `Ex18_E(b.operator double())`, or `Ex18_A(static_cast<A&>(b))`, but those shouldn't be considered for implicit conversion (and aren't, in C++) because `Ex18_E(const double&)` and `Ex18_E(const Ex18_A&)` are both marked explicit.
